coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence assay for bleomycin based on dual-emissive chameleon DNA-templated silver nanoclusters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Shuyun
Liu, Lingyuan
Sun, Jing
Shi, Fengjin
Zhao, Xian-En
coredata.dc:description: The authors design dual-emissive DNA-templated silver nanoclusters (DNA-AgNCs) for ratiometric fluorescence sensing bleomycin (BLM) for the first time. A hairpin probe containing two different C-rich DNA templates at two terminals is used to synthesize chameleon DNA-AgNCs, which possess two emission peaks when they are in close proximity. A strong emission is founded at 622 nm (λex = 570 nm) while a weak one is located at 572 nm (λex = 504 nm). Meanwhile, the loop of this probe contains the scission site (5′-GC-3′) of BLM. The loop can be cleaved into two parts by BLM-Fe(II) complex, inducing the two DNA-AgNCs away from each other. The fluorescence intensity at 572 nm and 622 nm increases and decreases, respectively. Such chameleon DNA-AgNCs exhibit an obvious fluorescence discoloration from orange to yellow. Therefore, a sensitive ratiometric fluorescent strategy for BLM detection has been proposed with the detection limit of 67 pM. Finally, this ratiometric method is used to detect BLM in serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA-templated silver nanoclusters
Chameleon
Ratiometric nanoprobe
Single fluorophore
Bleomycin
DNA scission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility study for ratiometric detection of BLM and possible mechanism
Detection sensitivity of the ratiometric assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is kindly supported by the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (No. ZR2020MB066 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The low-magnification, (B) high-magnification TEM images, (C) energy dispersive spectroscopic (EDS) and (D) the optical spectra of the chameleon DNA-AgNCs (curve a: absorption spectrum, b and d: excitation spectra, c and e: emission spectra under the excitation at 570 nm and 504 nm, respectively).

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of the chameleon DNA-AgNCs in the absence (a) and presence of 200 nM BLM-Fe(II) (b), 200 nM BLM (c), and 200 nM Fe(II) (d) under the excitation of 504 nm (left) and 570 nm (right), respectively. Inset: The corresponding photos under ultraviolet light. (B) 12% PAGE analysis of hairpin DNA in the absence (Lane 1) and presence of BLM-Fe(II) (Lane 2), BLM (Lane 3), and Fe(II) (Lane 4), respectively. The concentration of DNA is 1 μM.

Effects of DNA-AgNCs volume (A) and pH (B) and the fluorescence intensity ratio F 572/F 622 of DNA-AgNCs without (black column) and with 200 nM BLM-Fe (II) (red column). (C) The effect of ratio of BLM to Fe(II) on the detection of 200 nM BLM. The concentration of Fe(II) is 0, 50, 100, 200, 300 and 400 nM, respectively. (D) The effect of incubation time between DNA-AgNCs and BLM-Fe(II) on the detection of BLM, [BLM-Fe(II)] = 200 nM.

Fluorescence emission spectra (A) and the ratio F 572/F 622 (B) of the chameleon DNA-AgNCs with various concentrations of BLM-Fe(II) (0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, 20, 40, 100, 140, 200, 300, 400, 600, 800, 1000 nM).
Responses of the chameleon DNA-AgNCs for BLM and other antibiotics. [BLM] = 200 nM; [interferent] = 500 nM.
Schematic illustration of ratiometric fluorescence assay for BLM based on chameleon DNA-AgNCs.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for the detection of BLM.
Analytical results of human serum samples.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence assay for bleomycin based on dual-emissive chameleon DNA-templated silver nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for BLM has been designed based on chameleon DNA-AgNCs with dual fluorescence for the first time.

The authors design dual-emissive DNA-templated silver nanoclusters (DNA-AgNCs) for ratiometric fluorescence sensing bleomycin (BLM) for the first time. A hairpin probe containing two different C-rich DNA templates at two terminals is used to synthesize chameleon DNA-AgNCs, which possess two emission peaks when they are in close proximity. A strong emission is founded at 622 nm (λex = 570 nm) while a weak one is located at 572 nm (λex = 504 nm). Meanwhile, the loop of this probe contains the scission site (5′-GC-3′) of BLM. The loop can be cleaved into two parts by BLM-Fe(II) complex, inducing the two DNA-AgNCs away from each other. The fluorescence intensity at 572 nm and 622 nm increases and decreases, respectively. Such chameleon DNA-AgNCs exhibit an obvious fluorescence discoloration from orange to yellow. Therefore, a sensitive ratiometric fluorescent strategy for BLM detection has been proposed with the detection limit of 67 pM. Finally, this ratiometric method is used to detect BLM in serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has been one of the major threats to human health for centuries. The deaths from cancer are increasing year by year all over the world [1]. So, cancer therapy technology becomes extremely important. The antitumor drugs play important roles in cancer treatment [2]. As a member of glycopeptide-derived antibiotics, bleomycin sulphate (BLM) is prominent and has the specific advantages including low myelosuppression as well as low immunosuppression [3,4]. BLM plays important roles in the treatment of some cancers [5]. Although it is less toxic, overdose of BLM may lead to pulmonary fibrosis as well as pneumonitis [6]. Therefore, various techniques including electrochemical detection [7], colorimetric assay [8], fluorescent analysis [1,2], radioimmunoassay (RIA) [9] and so on have been used for quantitative detection of BLM.

Among them, fluorescence methods are widely concerned for their merits of easy operation and high sensitivity [10–13]. It has been reported that BLM exhibits cleavage activity towards DNA at 5′-GC/T-3′ sites with the assistance of Fe(II) [14]. Based on this fact, some fluorescent strategies have been designed for the detection of BLM by using various probes. For example, fluorescein (FAM)-labeled ssDNA combined with nanoquenchers including graphene oxide (GO) and WS2 nanosheets was used for fluorescent detection of BLM [2,15]. A BLM-Fe(II) triggered signal amplification was proposed for ultrasensitive detection of BLM with FAM fluorophore and a black hole quencher co-labeled DNA probe [16]. Qu’s group established a sensitive BLM assay based on GQDs-AuNPs nanocomposites connected by DNA [17]. Su’s group proposed a label-free method to detect BLM with target-mediated interaction between N-GQDs and ssDNA [18]. Zhang and coworkers applied BLM-initiated fluorescent copper nanoparticles to label-free detection of BLM [19].

However, these sensing strategies are single-wavelength-based detecting mode, which is often inevitably disturbed by interferences originating from experimental and/or environmental conditions. In contrast, ratiometric strategy can avoid the above problems efficiently and thus improve the sensitivity and accuracy [20–23]. Generally, ratiometric fluorescence technique need to measure the fluorescence intensity ratio of two well-resolved wavelengths. Ratiometric fluorescence strategies have drawn great attention in biochemical fields because of their strong anti-interference ability [24–28]. However, the literatures about ratiometric fluorescence assay for BLM are still rare. Su and coworkers established NGQDs@AuNCs assembly with dual-emission for ratiometric fluorescent detection of BLM [29]. Nevertheless, the usage of two different types of fluorescent nanomaterials make this assay complex. It is necessary to design dual emissive nanoprobe for establishing simple ratiometric sensors.

DNA-templated silver nanoclusters (DNA-AgNCs) have drawn great interesting due to their tunable synthesis [30–34]. Their fluorescence properties are dependent on DNA template as well as surrounding conditions. Interestingly, chameleon DNA-AgNCs have provided a new alternative for ratiometric sensing due to their multiple emission peaks [35–43]. For example, Donnell and coworkers designed a DNA-AgNC-based ratiometric DNA sensor with target-triggered shifts of fluorescence emission peaks [35]. Li’s group designed green-to-red fluorescence-converted chameleon DNA-AgNCs for ratiometric detection of DNA [36,37]. Red-to-yellow converted chameleon DNA-AgNCs coupled hybridization chain reaction were designed for ratiometric determination of miRNA [38]. Dong’s group constructed a novel molecular beacon templated AgNCs with dual fluorescence emission for the ratiometric detection of DNA [39]. Liu’s group applied dual-emission DNA-AgNCs for ratiometric detection of Pb2+, histidine, and cysteine [40,41]. Chameleon DNA-AgNCs with two-color (red and yellow) emissive fluorescence coupled with recycling amplification was designed for ratiometric detection of human papillomavirus-16 DNA [42]. Our group applied proximity-dependent DNA-AgNCs for ratiometric fluorescence detection of BLM [43]. However, the ratiometric fluorescence assay for BLM based on dual-emissive DNA-AgNCs is still rare.

We herein propose a chameleon DNA-AgNC-based ratiometric strategy for sensitive determination of BLM in combination with the DNA scission of BLM. As shown in Scheme 1 , a hairpin DNA probe containing two different C-rich scaffolds at two terminals is used to synthesize DNA-AgNCs. Due to the proximity of the two AgNCs, the chameleon DNA-AgNCs show a strong emission at 622 nm (λex = 570 nm) and a weak one at 572 nm (λex = 504 nm). Meanwhile, the loop of this probe contains a scission site (5′-GC-3′) for BLM. When BLM-Fe(II) complex is present, the hairpin probe is cleaved into two parts, inducing two DNA-AgNCs away from each other. An enhanced yellow fluorescence at 572 nm is observed followed by a decrease of fluorescence at 622 nm. The dramatic fluorescence color conversion from orange to yellow is caused by the BLM-Fe(II) complex. Therefore, a novel and sensitive method for ratiometric detection of BLM has been proposed by recording the intensity ratio F 572/F 622. The detection limit is as low as 67 pM. Moreover, this method displays high selectivity and is successfully applied to the analysis of BLM in serum samples.

Oligonucleotide (5′-CCCTTAATCCCCTTTTCTCACAGCTACATACTCCATAGAAAACCCCCTAATTCCCCC-3′, HPLC), DNA marker, loading buffer and 5 × Tris/borate/EDTA (TBE) buffer were provided by Sangon Biological Engineering Technology Co. Ltd., (Shanghai, China). Sliver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), and the other metal salts were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Beomycin (BLM), dactinomycin, mitomycin, and daunorubicin were bought from Melone Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd. (Dalian, China). Kanamycin, vancomycin, and penicillin were provided by Beijing Dingguo Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water (18 MΩ•cm−1) was used throughout the experiments.

All fluorescence spectra were collected on F-7100 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The PMT voltage was 600 V and both excitation and emission slit widths were set at 10.0 nm. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were carried on a JEM-2100Plus microscope. UV–vis spectra were performed on a spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600, Japan). Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded on a MOS-500 spectropolarimeter (Bio-Logic, France). The image of gel electrophoresis was scanned by the gel imaging analysis system (Tanon 4600 SF, Shanghai).

According to previous report [44], chameleon DNA-AgNCs were synthesized as follows: 100 μM DNA (60 μL) and 1 mM AgNO3 (60 μL) were mixed in phosphate buffer (10 mM, 1820 μL, pH 7.5) and then incubated at 4 ℃ in dark. After 1 h, 1 mM NaBH4 solution (60 μL) was added with vigorous shaking for 2 min. Finally, the mixture was reacted at 4 ℃ for 3 h before use.

50 μL of DNA-AgNCs and 10 μL BLM-Fe(II) with varied concentrations were mixed together in 140 μL phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). After reaction for 40 min, the fluorescence spectra under the excitation of 570 nm and 504 nm were measured, respectively. The fluorescence intensities at 622 nm as well as 572 nm were recorded, respectively. The fluorescence intensity ratio of F 572/F 622 were used as an output signal to analyze BLM.

Nondenaturating polyacrylamine gel electrophoresis (PAGE) was used to further verify the cleavage of hairpin DNA by BLM-Fe(II). The concentration of each standard DNA sample was set as 1 μM. A mixture of each DNA sample (10 μL) and loading buffer (3 μL) was added into 12% polyacrylamide gel. All the prepared gels were run at a constant voltage of 120 V for 45 min in 1 × TBE buffer followed by staining with 4S Red Plus for 30 min and imaged with a gel imaging system.

Human serum samples were provided by healthy adults from Qufu People’s Hospital. For eliminating protein interference, acetonitrile was added followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm. After 15 min, the supernate was diluted 100 times and then analyzed by this ratiometric method.

The morphology of chameleon DNA-AgNCs was obtained with TEM. As presented in Fig. 1 A, a typically spherical DNA-AgNCs is observed with an average diameter of 4.6 nm. The high-magnification image indicates that the DNA-AgNCs are well separated (Fig. 1B). From energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), an absorption peak at 3 KeV is observed, suggesting the formation of metallic AgNCs [45] (Fig. 1C). Fig. 1D shows the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of chameleon DNA-AgNCs. There are two absorption peaks at 400 nm and 560 nm, respectively, which are in agreement with the reported literature [44]. The peak at 400 nm is the characteristic plasmon absorption band of Ag nanoparticles [45,46] while the peak at 560 nm corresponds to the absorption of DNA-AgNCs. The TEM image of DNA-AgNCs indicates the presence of a small amount of Ag nanoparticles (Fig. S1), in accordance with the UV–vis spectroscopy results. As expected, there is a strong emission peak at 622 nm under excitation at 570 nm (Fig. 1D, curve b,c), while no fluorescence emission occurrs upon excitation at 400 nm (Fig. S2). Meanwhile, there is a weak emission at 572 nm under the excitation at 504 nm (Fig. 1D, curve d,e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility study for ratiometric detection of BLM and possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 A shows the fluorescence emission spectra of chameleon DNA-AgNCs under different conditions. Chameleon DNA-AgNCs exhibit a strong emission at 622 nm and a weak one at 572 nm, respectively (curve a). When BLM-Fe(II) complex is added, the fluorescence intensity at 572 nm increases remarkably followed by the reduction of fluorescence at 622 nm (curve b). However, fluorescence spectra of chameleon DNA-AgNCs change slightly when either BLM or Fe(II) is added (curve c,d). The corresponding digital images under ultraviolet light (Inset of Fig. 2A) also reveal the color conversion from orange to yellow upon the addition of BLM-Fe(II). Nevertheless, the addition of either BLM or Fe(II) has slight influence on the color of DNA-AgNCs.

To further validate the cleavage of 5′-GC-3′ site in the loop region of hairpin DNA by BLM-Fe(II), the experiment of nondenaturing polyacrylamide gel analysis (PAGE) analysis was performed and the results were shown in Fig. 1B. Lane 1 is the band of hairpin DNA. For Lane 2, there is an extra band corresponding to the scission product 5′-CTACATACTCCATAGAAAACCCCCTAATTCCCCC-3′ induced by the cleavage of BLM-Fe(II). However, the presence of neither BLM (Lane 3) nor Fe(II) (Lane 4) induces the obvious cleavage of hairpin DNA. Furthermore, circular dichroism (CD) spectra were also performed to verify the successful cleavage with the results presented in Fig. S3. For chameleon DNA-AgNCs, a positive peak at 278 nm and a negative one at 246 nm are observed. When BLM-Fe(II) is added, a positive peak at 285 nm and a negative peak at 252 nm are observed, indicating the effective oxidation scission of the hairpin DNA. In addition, the size of DNA-AgNCs after treating with BLM-Fe(II) was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. S4, the average diameter of DNA-AgNCs after treating with BLM-Fe(II) is 3.12 nm, which is smaller than that of DNA-AgNCs before treating with BLM-Fe(II) (4.6 nm).

According to the previous report [44], the DNA-AgNCs templated by 5′-CCCTTAATCCCC-3′ were nonfluorescent while those templated by 5′-CCCCCTAATTCCCCC-3′ presented strong fluorescence emission at 570 nm. Herein, due to the hairpin DNA, the two different DNA-AgNCs are assembled within nanoscale proximity and unite together to form a new type of AgNCs with a larger size, which is confirmed by TEM (Fig. 1A and Fig. S4). The new type of DNA-AgNCs possess dual fluorescence properties: a strong emission at 622 nm and a weak one at 572 nm. The addition of BLM-Fe(II) can selectively cleave the loop of the hairpin into two parts at the recognition site, keeping two DNA-AgNCs away from each other. It has been reported that the assembling of two C-rich templates in proximity is essential for dual emissive fluorescence DNA-AgNCs [44]. If the two templates only mix in solution without interaction, double fluorescence cannot be obtained. Thus, the fluorescence intensity at 572 nm increases remarkably followed by the reduction of fluorescence at 622 nm. Moreover, the change of microenvironmental due to the proximity of the two DNA-AgNCs can also cause the dramatic variation of fluorescence properties.

To obtain the optimal sensing performance, some experimental parameters were optimized prior analysis. First of all, the stability of DNA-AgNCs was investigated with the results shown in Fig. S5. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensities of DNA-AgNCs both at 572 nm and 622 nm are stable within 2 h under room temperature (Fig. S5A). The fluorescence ratios F 572/F 622 also change slightly within 2 h under room temperature (Fig. S5B). If keeping DNA-AgNCs at 4 ℃, the fluorescence intensities both at 572 nm and 622 nm are stable even for 10 h (Fig. S5C). Similarly, the fluorescence ratios F 572/F 622 change slightly during these 10 h (Fig. S5D). Thus, the storage of DNA-AgNCs is kept at 4 ℃ after synthesis.

Then, we studied the amount of DNA-AgNCs on the responses of BLM. Fig. 3 A shows that the fluorescence ratios F 572/F 622 of chameleon DNA-AgNCs change slightly with various volume of DNA-AgNCs (black column). After adding BLM-Fe(II) complex, the fluorescence ratios F 572/F 622 decrease gradually with the increasing volume of DNA-AgNCs (red column). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensities of DNA-AgNCs at both emissive peaks decrease remarkably with the decreasing volume of DNA-AgNCs (Fig. S6). Too weak signal is not facile for analysis. Thus, moderate 50 μL DNA-AgNCs is chosen to obtain both strong fluorescence signals and high fluorescence ratio.

The influence of pH on the sensing system was also studied with the results presented in Fig. 3B. The results indicate that pH value of solution has slight influence on fluorescence ratio F 572/F 622 of chameleon DNA-AgNCs. After the addition of BLM-Fe(II) complex, the fluorescence ratio F 572/F 622 increases gradually and then decreases with the increasing pH. The fluorescence ratio F 572/F 622 reaches maximum at pH 7.5, which is used in the following experiments.

It has been reported that a reduced metal is necessary for DNA scission by BLM. The effects of metal ions on DNA scission were studied with the results displayed in Fig. S7. It can be seen that the fluorescence ratios F 572/F 622 of DNA-AgNCs change slightly in the presence of BLM with other metal ions (Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Mg2+ or Cu2+). As expected, BLM changes the fluorescence ratio F 572/F 622 remarkably with the assistance of Fe2+. Then, we investigated the influence of ratio of BLM to Fe(II) on the detection of BLM. Fig. 3C shows the ratios F 572/F 622 of DNA-AgNCs in the presence of 200 nM BLM and different concentration of Fe(II). It can be seen that the best fluorescence ratio F 572/F 622 is obtained when the ratio of BLM to Fe(II) is 1:1.

Fig. 3D shows the effect of incubation time between DNA-AgNCs and BLM-Fe(II) on the response of BLM. The fluorescence ratios F 572/F 622 increase gradually with the increasing time and then reach the maximum at 40 min, which is chosen for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection sensitivity of the ratiometric assay
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of this ratiometric assay for BLM was studied with the results shown in Fig. 4 . The fluorescence emission spectra of chameleon DNA-AgNCs were recorded after the addition of BLM-Fe(II) with varied concentrations (Fig. 4A). The fluorescence intensities at 622 nm decrease gradually while those at 572 nm increase simultaneously as BLM concentrations increase. Fig. 4B shows the fluorescence ratio F 572/F 622 presents a linear relationship against BLM concentration from 0.2 nM to 1000 nM. The regression equation is F 572/F 622 = 0.3862 + 0.05008C (R2 = 0.9908). The detection of limit is as low as 67 pM (S/N = 3), which is one order of magnitude lower than those of most reported methods (Table 1 ). Moreover, this ratiometric fluorescence system is constructed with one nanoprobe, which is more convenient than those based on two types of nanoprobes.

High selectivity is another crucial factor for construction of sensors. Fig. 5 shows that other antibiotics including daunorubicin, mitomycin, dactinomycin, kanamycin, vancomycin and penicillin have negligible influences on the ratio F 572/F 622. In contrast, the presence of BLM induces a remarkable change on the ratio F 572/F 622. The high selectivity of this ratiometric method is mainly caused by the selective scission of BLM towards DNA.

To confirm the validity of this strategy in practical application, the recovery experiments in human serum samples are performed by standard addition method. As presented in Table 2 , the recoveries vary from 92.0% to 107.6% and the RSD is from 2.4% to 4.1%. These results illustrate the feasibility of chameleon DNA-AgNC-based ratiometric method in the practical applications.

A ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for BLM detection has been developed with chameleon DNA-AgNCs as signal probe. BLM-Fe(II) complex can cleave the hairpin probe into two parts, inducing the two C-rich DNA-AgNCs away from each other. The dual-emissive DNA-AgNCs exhibit target-trigged obvious discoloration from orange to yellow emission. This ratiometric nanoprobe can improve the detection accuracy through reducing false-negative as well as false-positive responses. The selective scission of BLM towards DNA make the ratiometric nanoprobe good selectivity. This ratiometric system uses DNA-AgNCs as single fluorophore, which makes this method more convenient in preparation and operation.

Shuyun Zhu: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Lingyuan Liu: Validation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Jing Sun: Software. Fengjin Shi: Data curation. Xian-En Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is kindly supported by the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (No. ZR2020MB066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119521.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A G-triplex based molecular beacon for label-free fluorescence “turn-on” detection of bleomycin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Ma, X. Han, L. Xia, R.M. Kong, F.L. Qu, A G-triplex based molecular beacon for label-free fluorescence “turn-on” detection of bleomycin, Analyst 143 (2018) 5474-5780.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive fluorescence turn-on assay of bleomycin and nuclease using WS2 nanosheet as an effective sensing platform

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Qin, Y. Ma, X. Jin, L. Zhang, G. Ye, S. Zhao, A sensitive fluorescence turn-on assay of bleomycin and nuclease using WS2 nanosheet as an effective sensing platform, Anal. Chim. Acta 866 (2015) 84-89.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dynamics of bleomycin interaction with a strongly bound hairpin DNA substrate, and implications for cleavage of the bound DNA

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.C. Bozeman, R. Nanjunda, C. Tang, Y. Liu, Z.J. Segerman, P.A. Zaleski, Wilson W.D., Hecht S.M., Dynamics of bleomycin interaction with a strongly bound hairpin DNA substrate, and implications for cleavage of the bound DNA, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 134 (2012) 17842-17845.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Marked immunosuppression with minimal myelosuppression by bleomycin in vitro

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Tisman, V. Herbert, L.T. Go, L. Brenner, Marked immunosuppression with minimal myelosuppression by bleomycin in vitro, Blood 41 (1973) 721-726.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antitumor antibiotics: bleomycin, enediynes, and mitomycin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U. Galm, M.H. Hager, S.G. Van Lanen, J. Ju, J.S. Thorson, B. Shen, Antitumor antibiotics: bleomycin, enediynes, and mitomycin, Chem. Rev. 105 (2005) 739-758.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bleomycin lung toxicity: who are the patients with increased risk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Azambuja, J.F. Fleck, R.G. Batista, S.S.M. Barreto, Bleomycin lung toxicity: who are the patients with increased risk. Pulm. Pharmacol. Ther. 18 (2005) 363-366.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive DNA-based electrochemical strategy for trace bleomycin detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yin, D. Wu, B. Ye, Sensitive DNA-based electrochemical strategy for trace bleomycin detection, Anal. Chem. 82 (2010) 8272-8277.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple colorimetric sensing of trace bleomycin using unmodified gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Li, Y. Feng, C. Zhao, B. Tang, Simple colorimetric sensing of trace bleomycin using unmodified gold nanoparticles. Biosens. Bioelectron. 26 (2011) 4628-4631.
A. Broughton, J.E. Strong, Radioimmunoassay of bleomycin, Cancer Res. 36 (1976) 1418-1421.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Turn-on fluorescent assay for antioxidants based on their inhibiting polymerization of dopamine on graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Zhu, X.L. Yan, J.Y. Qiu, J. Sun, X.E. Zhao, Turn-on fluorescent assay for antioxidants based on their inhibiting polymerization of dopamine on graphene quantum dots, Spectrochim. Acta A 225 (2020) 117516.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Probing glutathione reductase activity with graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.L. Yan, X.E. Zhao, J. Sun, S.Y. Zhu, C.H. Lei, R. Li, P.W. Gong, B.P. Ling, R.J. Wang, H. Wang, Probing glutathione reductase activity with graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles, Sensor. Actuat. B 263 (2018) 27-35.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel and sensitive fluorescent assay for artemisinin with graphene quantum dots based on inner filter effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Zhu, X.L. Yan, J. Sun, X.E. Zhao, X. Wang, A novel and sensitive fluorescent assay for artemisinin with graphene quantum dots based on inner filter effect, Talanta 200 (2019) 163-168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of intercalating molecules in detection of methylated DNA in the presence of silver ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Soltaninejad, A.A. Sadeghan, S. Hosseinkahani, M.A. Asadollahi, M. Hosseini, M.R. Ganjali, Application of intercalating molecules in detection of methylated DNA in the presence of silver ions, Methods Appl. Fluores. 7 (2019) 035005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization of bleomycin cleavage sites in strongly bound hairpin DNAs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.A. Giroux, S.M. Hecht, Characterization of bleomycin cleavage sites in strongly bound hairpin DNAs, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 132 (2010) 16987-16996.
